question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 204km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (30kg each) and 5 units of construction material (78kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 209km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of relief supply box (32kg each) and 5 units of construction material (74kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 27%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 79 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 53 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 53min, 2nd: 68min, 3rd: 83min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 183km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of construction material (66kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 27%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 83min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (58kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (42kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 99km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 61 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 61min, 2nd: 77min, 3rd: 93min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 217km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (75kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 27km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 220km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (48kg each) and 4 units of construction material (76kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 120km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 80min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (45kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 176km waypoint, 8 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 89 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 70min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 219km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (48kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 117km waypoint, 8 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 207km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of construction material (64kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (43kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

At the 60km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 58 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 58min, 2nd: 74min, 3rd: 90min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 206km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (53kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (48kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 132km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 58 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 58min, 2nd: 75min, 3rd: 92min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (43kg each) and 8 units of construction material (74kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 120km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 56min, 3rd: 72min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 211km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (80kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (22kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 156km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 60min, 3rd: 77min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 220km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (31kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 39km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 68min, 3rd: 82min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 208km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 8 units of construction material (64kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 165km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 78min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 228km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (68kg each) and 8 units of medical kit (9kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 176km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 61 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 61min, 2nd: 76min, 3rd: 91min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 196km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (80kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 59min, 3rd: 73min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 308km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (56kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (14kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 78min, 3rd: 106min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 320km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 21 units of construction material (68kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 23km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 250km waypoint, 13 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 38 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 92min, 3rd: 130min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 294km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (21kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 60km waypoint, 8 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 91min, 3rd: 128min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 294km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (77kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 88km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 97min, 3rd: 134min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 355km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of construction material (76kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (7kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 39km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 210km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 110 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 76 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 76min, 2nd: 103min, 3rd: 130min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 329km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of water barrel (19kg each) and 10 units of construction material (61kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 40km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 315km waypoint, 6 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 67 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 67min, 2nd: 98min, 3rd: 129min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 315km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (7kg each) and 4 units of construction material (71kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 286km waypoint, 1 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 99 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 53 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 53min, 2nd: 87min, 3rd: 121min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 310km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 19 units of construction material (68kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 260km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 110 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 75 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 38 minutes (1st: 75min, 2nd: 113min, 3rd: 151min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 295km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of medical kit (6kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (68kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 70km waypoint, 10 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 99 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 93min, 3rd: 123min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 311km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of water barrel (17kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (68kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 108km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 111 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 76 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 76min, 2nd: 107min, 3rd: 138min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 319km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (62kg each) and 10 units of medical kit (14kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 39km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 189km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 77 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 77min, 2nd: 108min, 3rd: 139min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 326km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 20 units of water barrel (16kg each) and 9 units of construction material (75kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 230km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 88min, 3rd: 116min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 336km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 4 units of construction material (53kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 40km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 77km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 109 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 53 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 53min, 2nd: 82min, 3rd: 111min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 316km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (33kg each) and 7 units of construction material (74kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 27km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 288km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 79 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 79min, 2nd: 112min, 3rd: 145min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 351km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of water barrel (17kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (32kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 80km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 111 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 85min, 3rd: 122min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (79kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (25kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 252km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 95 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 82 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 82min, 2nd: 115min, 3rd: 148min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 324km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (59kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (8kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 42km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 234km waypoint, 9 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 104 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 84min, 3rd: 111min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 361km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 22 units of construction material (68kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (35kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 344km waypoint, 10 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 90min, 3rd: 126min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:

  - Congestion: 9:00 AM~2:00 PM, Zone B -34%
  - Mid-journey delivery: 344km, construction material x10
  - Cumulative fatigue: +36min per rest

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 337km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 20 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (53kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 99km waypoint, 8 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 107 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 78min, 3rd: 111min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of water barrel (15kg each) and 7 units of construction material (67kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 60km waypoint, 8 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 119 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 74 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 74min, 2nd: 103min, 3rd: 132min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 335km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 19 units of construction material (66kg each) and 10 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 300km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 110 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 75 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 75min, 2nd: 102min, 3rd: 129min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 348km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (63kg each) and 9 units of relief supply box (43kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 198km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 94min, 3rd: 131min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 331km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (63kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 39km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 140km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 120 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 78min, 3rd: 105min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 367km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (54kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (61kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 42km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 140km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 80 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 80min, 2nd: 110min, 3rd: 140min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 320km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (57kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (15kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 44km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 73 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 73min, 2nd: 102min, 3rd: 131min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 355km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (30kg each) and 9 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 40%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 160km waypoint, 8 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 68 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 68min, 2nd: 104min, 3rd: 140min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 311km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of medical kit (6kg each) and 7 units of construction material (77kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 120km waypoint, 9 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 53 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 53min, 2nd: 82min, 3rd: 111min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 347km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of water barrel (25kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 216km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 78 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 78min, 2nd: 106min, 3rd: 134min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
