question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 362km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of water barrel (17kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (48kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 43km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 140km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 53 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 53min, 2nd: 86min, 3rd: 119min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 320km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 20 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 8 units of construction material (73kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 36km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 261km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 109 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 80 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 80min, 2nd: 112min, 3rd: 144min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of medical kit (13kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (41kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 42km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 126km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 74min, 3rd: 103min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 353km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 5 units of construction material (58kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 120km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 107 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 84 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 84min, 2nd: 117min, 3rd: 150min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 293km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (76kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (62kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 70km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 66 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 66min, 2nd: 93min, 3rd: 120min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 363km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 10 units of construction material (79kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 40km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 100km waypoint, 8 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 75min, 3rd: 102min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 329km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 39km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 120km waypoint, 7 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 101 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 94min, 3rd: 125min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of water barrel (19kg each) and 5 units of construction material (65kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 66km waypoint, 9 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 118 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 84min, 3rd: 117min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (64kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 130km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 107 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 80min, 3rd: 111min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 298km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 19 units of construction material (50kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (62kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 210km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 104 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 70 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 70min, 2nd: 98min, 3rd: 126min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 323km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (67kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 300km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 84min, 3rd: 121min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 359km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (54kg each) and 7 units of construction material (76kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 328km waypoint, 7 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 109 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 70 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 70min, 2nd: 105min, 3rd: 140min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 339km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (62kg each) and 10 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 154km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 113 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 85 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 85min, 2nd: 122min, 3rd: 159min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 326km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (66kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (11kg each). The boat can travel at 22km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 39km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 132km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 77 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 77min, 2nd: 107min, 3rd: 137min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 333km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (66kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (15kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 44km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 290km waypoint, 7 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 79min, 3rd: 110min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 292km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 22 units of relief supply box (67kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 40km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 59 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 59min, 2nd: 89min, 3rd: 119min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 303km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of water barrel (21kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (61kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 81km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 103 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 79 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 79min, 2nd: 116min, 3rd: 153min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 309km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (33kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 261km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 38 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 85min, 3rd: 123min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 360km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (65kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 253km waypoint, 9 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 80 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 38 minutes (1st: 80min, 2nd: 118min, 3rd: 156min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 320km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 19 units of water barrel (19kg each) and 10 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 40%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 290km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 119 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 77 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 77min, 2nd: 107min, 3rd: 137min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 320km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 19 units of medical kit (6kg each) and 9 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 117km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 105 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 74 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 38 minutes (1st: 74min, 2nd: 112min, 3rd: 150min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 342km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of water barrel (16kg each) and 4 units of construction material (63kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 250km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 70 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 70min, 2nd: 103min, 3rd: 136min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 339km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 21 units of construction material (75kg each) and 8 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 60km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 113 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 83min, 3rd: 114min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 329km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (22kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 264km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 56 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 56min, 2nd: 83min, 3rd: 110min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 357km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (42kg each) and 5 units of construction material (61kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 121km waypoint, 8 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 38 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 83min, 3rd: 121min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 322km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (66kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 40%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 10 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 76 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 76min, 2nd: 108min, 3rd: 140min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 332km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (59kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (59kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 231km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 113 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 74 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 74min, 2nd: 101min, 3rd: 128min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 311km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 9 units of construction material (64kg each). The boat can travel at 22km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 121km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 69 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 69min, 2nd: 100min, 3rd: 131min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 313km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (66kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (15kg each). The boat can travel at 23km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 39%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 56 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 56min, 2nd: 89min, 3rd: 122min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 346km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (57kg each) and 7 units of construction material (73kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 253km waypoint, 11 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 120 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 66 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 66min, 2nd: 96min, 3rd: 126min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 331km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (61kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (42kg each). The boat can travel at 20km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 99km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 99 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 69 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 69min, 2nd: 105min, 3rd: 141min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 369km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 4 units of construction material (70kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 44km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 40%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 330km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 102 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 79min, 3rd: 110min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 298km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (75kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 118 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 82 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 82min, 2nd: 116min, 3rd: 150min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 303km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (66kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 77km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 119 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 83 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 83min, 2nd: 118min, 3rd: 153min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 359km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (30kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (8kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 39km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 110km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 117 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 74 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 74min, 2nd: 105min, 3rd: 136min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 318km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 21 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 9 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 36km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 11:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 286km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 109 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 64 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 64min, 2nd: 100min, 3rd: 136min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 302km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 20 units of construction material (60kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 80km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 114 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 64 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 64min, 2nd: 96min, 3rd: 128min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 308km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of water barrel (21kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (35kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 224km waypoint, 7 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 101 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 83min, 3rd: 120min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 320km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of medical kit (6kg each) and 7 units of construction material (71kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 66km waypoint, 11 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 101 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 81min, 3rd: 110min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 335km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 5 units of construction material (77kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 253km waypoint, 10 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 56 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 56min, 2nd: 85min, 3rd: 114min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 371km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 19 units of construction material (56kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 24km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 154km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 84 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 84min, 2nd: 111min, 3rd: 138min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 313km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (7kg each). The boat can travel at 20km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 17km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 66km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 100 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 79 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 79min, 2nd: 110min, 3rd: 141min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 351km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of water barrel (22kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (11kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 144km waypoint, 10 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 85 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 85min, 2nd: 120min, 3rd: 155min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 317km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 21 units of relief supply box (54kg each) and 10 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 43km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 290km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 118 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 70 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 70min, 2nd: 100min, 3rd: 130min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
