question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 319km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (55kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 110km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 107 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 80min, 3rd: 112min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 320km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 21 units of relief supply box (61kg each) and 8 units of construction material (69kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 198km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 78 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 78min, 2nd: 110min, 3rd: 142min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 327km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 21 units of construction material (55kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (62kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 70km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 27 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 74min, 3rd: 101min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 314km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 19 units of medical kit (10kg each) and 9 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 23km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 18km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 55km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 81 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 81min, 2nd: 111min, 3rd: 141min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 329km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (78kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 37%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 297km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 101 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 67 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 36 minutes (1st: 67min, 2nd: 103min, 3rd: 139min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 347km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (70kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 130km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 55 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 55min, 2nd: 84min, 3rd: 113min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 368km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (13kg each) and 9 units of relief supply box (47kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 45km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 38%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 56km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 119 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 94min, 3rd: 128min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 313km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (52kg each) and 9 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 130km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 91min, 3rd: 122min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 338km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of relief supply box (62kg each) and 5 units of construction material (50kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 44km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 209km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 101 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 56 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 29 minutes (1st: 56min, 2nd: 85min, 3rd: 114min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 344km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 19 units of relief supply box (50kg each) and 8 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 8:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 319km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 112 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 76 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 76min, 2nd: 104min, 3rd: 132min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 322km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 20 units of relief supply box (34kg each) and 4 units of construction material (57kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 180km waypoint, 7 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 118 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 94min, 3rd: 125min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 312km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of water barrel (17kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (63kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 42km is Zone A (speed limit: 19km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 261km waypoint, 9 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 77 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 77min, 2nd: 107min, 3rd: 137min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 348km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 22 units of construction material (71kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (51kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 220km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 100 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 59 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 59min, 2nd: 94min, 3rd: 129min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 312km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (5kg each) and 10 units of construction material (52kg each). The boat can travel at 26km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 37km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 13km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 80km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 117 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 68 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 68min, 2nd: 100min, 3rd: 132min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 313km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 20 units of water barrel (23kg each) and 5 units of construction material (53kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 154km waypoint, 8 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 116 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 37 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 97min, 3rd: 134min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 323km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (74kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (68kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 36%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 72 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 72min, 2nd: 103min, 3rd: 134min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 290km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (58kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (41kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 8:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 32%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 225km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 100 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 34 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 94min, 3rd: 128min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 328km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 20 units of construction material (52kg each) and 10 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 34%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 33%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 230km waypoint, 11 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 119 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 75 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 75min, 2nd: 105min, 3rd: 135min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 343km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 23km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 38km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 38%.
From 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 200km waypoint, 6 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 108 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 83 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 35 minutes (1st: 83min, 2nd: 118min, 3rd: 153min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 345km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (54kg each) and 9 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 32%.
From 11:00 AM to 5:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 35%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 170km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 105 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 32 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 86min, 3rd: 118min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 337km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 19 units of construction material (54kg each) and 10 units of relief supply box (44kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 15km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 33%.
From 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 40%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 190km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 115 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 81 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 33 minutes (1st: 81min, 2nd: 114min, 3rd: 147min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 305km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (57kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (8kg each). The boat can travel at 25km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 14km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 35%.
From 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 270km waypoint, 7 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 106 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 31 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 83min, 3rd: 114min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 353km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (11kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 7km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 36%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 37%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 242km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 113 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 46 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 30 minutes (1st: 46min, 2nd: 76min, 3rd: 106min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 331km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (42kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 8km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 42km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 16km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 39%.
From 9:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 34%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 121km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 117 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 62 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 28 minutes (1st: 62min, 2nd: 90min, 3rd: 118min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
